questions.passage: When a writer's diary enters an archive, privacy changes tense. The locked drawer becomes a catalog entry; the embarrassment meant for no one becomes a source. Scholars defend this transformation by appealing to knowledge. Diaries reveal drafts of thought, unguarded prejudice, private grief, and the mundane circumstances from which public work emerged. Without them, biography risks becoming a smooth monument built from official statements and polished recollections. Yet the diary's value is bound to its violation. It interests us because it was not written as public performance, and that very fact makes its publication ethically unstable. Consent cannot be retrieved from the dead, and instructions left by the dead are not always decisive. A writer may burn some notebooks and preserve others, may ask for destruction in a mood of fear, or may assume that no future reader could care about an ordinary complaint written after dinner. The archive converts uncertainty into policy. Some readers try to solve the problem by distinguishing great writers from ordinary people. Genius, they suggest, creates a claim on privacy that belongs to history rather than to the person who kept the diary. This argument is both powerful and dangerous. It explains why private papers can illuminate a culture, but it also risks turning talent into forfeiture. To have written well in public should not mean that every private weakness becomes common property. The better defense of diary publication is narrower. An archive should ask what kind of understanding the material makes possible and what kind of exposure it merely satisfies. A notebook that clarifies the making of a major work, records a vanished social world, or challenges a self-protective public myth may justify careful access. A page that offers only humiliation asks curiosity to masquerade as scholarship. Even then, publication should preserve some friction. Editors can contextualize, withhold names, delay release, or admit uncertainty rather than pretending that archival availability equals moral permission. The diary reminds us that knowledge is not innocent simply because it is true. To read the private dead is sometimes necessary, sometimes illuminating, and sometimes only appetite with footnotes. The ethical question becomes sharper when a diary contains both public significance and private injury. A notebook may reveal the making of a poem, but the same page may expose a family quarrel whose participants never consented to become evidence. Editors sometimes solve this difficulty by waiting, redacting, or publishing selectively. None of these solutions is pure. Waiting may protect the living while distorting the historical record; redaction may preserve dignity while creating a misleading smoothness; full publication may serve scholarship while gratifying curiosity. The archive's responsibility is therefore not to pretend that access is automatically virtuous. It must ask what kind of knowledge is being sought and whether the reader's hunger has been disciplined by a reason stronger than possession. The reader's posture is therefore part of the ethical situation. To approach a diary as if every private sentence were owed to the public is to mistake access for entitlement. To refuse all reading, however, may protect privacy at the cost of flattening the life whose public work is being studied. Responsible reading occupies the uneasy space between these errors. It asks whether the private detail changes interpretation, whether harm can be reduced without falsifying the record, and whether the pleasure of intimacy has begun to masquerade as scholarship. The diary does not answer these questions for us. It forces us to admit that evidence can be both precious and improperly easy to desire.
questions.stem: The passage’s central claim about diaries in archives is that they:

choices.A: should never be read once their authors have died.
choices.B: are reliable only when they confirm an author’s public statements.
choices.C: belong entirely to scholars rather than to families or editors.
choices.D: offer knowledge through an ethically unstable loss of privacy.

explanations.A: The passage does not forbid reading diaries. It says the practice can be necessary or illuminating.
explanations.B: The passage values diaries partly because they complicate polished public records.
explanations.C: The archive has responsibility, but the passage does not give scholars absolute ownership.
explanations.D: Correct. This captures the central tension: diaries matter because they were private, and that privacy is what makes their use ethically unstable.

questions.stem: The claim that “privacy changes tense” means that:

choices.A: readers can restore privacy by studying a diary carefully.
choices.B: diary writers usually become more private as they grow older.
choices.C: a private text becomes public after consent conditions change.
choices.D: archives convert old writing into fiction by changing verb forms.

explanations.A: Careful study may be more responsible, but it does not undo the exposure.
explanations.B: The passage is not making a developmental claim about writers.
explanations.C: Correct. The phrase captures the shift from locked drawer to catalog entry. The writer’s privacy belongs to the past, but the consequences of reading occur in the present.
explanations.D: Grammar is metaphorical here; the archive changes access, not verb tense.

questions.stem: The discussion of redaction and waiting primarily serves to show that:

choices.A: protective policies involve tradeoffs rather than moral purity.
choices.B: editors can easily solve the ethics of archival publication.
choices.C: scholarship should always take precedence over embarrassment.
choices.D: families are better judges of diaries than scholars in every case.

explanations.A: Correct. Waiting, redaction, and full publication each preserve something while risking something else. The examples complicate any simple policy.
explanations.B: The passage says none of these solutions is pure, so they are not easy fixes.
explanations.C: Scholarship is important, but the passage resists treating access as automatically virtuous.
explanations.D: The passage does not assign universal authority to families.

questions.stem: Which editorial decision would best fit the passage’s standard?

choices.A: Redacting entries while preserving material needed for interpretation.
choices.B: Publishing every diary entry immediately because truth is always innocent.
choices.C: Destroying all private notebooks to avoid any possible embarrassment.
choices.D: Publishing only entries that make the writer appear admirable.

explanations.A: Correct. This answer preserves the passage’s balance: access should be disciplined by reasons, and protection should not falsify the record.
explanations.B: The passage explicitly denies that knowledge is innocent simply because it is true.
explanations.C: Total destruction would sacrifice historical understanding and ignore cases where diaries matter.
explanations.D: This would create the smooth monument the passage distrusts.

questions.stem: The author’s attitude toward scholarly curiosity is best described as:

choices.A: neutral because archives simply follow technical procedures.
choices.B: respectful when disciplined, suspicious as mere appetite.
choices.C: hostile because curiosity always violates the dead.
choices.D: uncritical because all private documents enrich biography.

explanations.A: Archive policy is treated as ethically charged, not merely technical.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage values diaries for knowledge, but warns that curiosity can become appetite with footnotes. The standard is disciplined purpose.
explanations.C: The author does not condemn all curiosity. Some private writing may be necessary for understanding.
explanations.D: The passage rejects automatic virtue in access.

questions.stem: Which finding would most strengthen the passage’s caution?

choices.A: Some readers find published diaries more engaging than official biographies.
choices.B: Published diary passages harm living relatives while adding little value.
choices.C: A diary reveals how a major public work developed over several years.
choices.D: Editors often include notes explaining names and historical references.

explanations.A: Reader engagement may reflect appetite rather than justified knowledge.
explanations.B: Correct. This directly supports the argument that access must be justified by reasons stronger than possession or curiosity.
explanations.C: This supports the value side of the tension, not the caution.
explanations.D: Notes may improve understanding, but do not address the privacy problem.

questions.stem: The author would most likely reject which claim?

choices.A: A diary’s value may depend on the fact that it was not written for public performance.
choices.B: Redaction can protect dignity while risking distortion of the record.
choices.C: Once a document is historically interesting, privacy no longer matters.
choices.D: The dead may leave instructions that are difficult to interpret responsibly.

explanations.A: This aligns with the passage’s central paradox.
explanations.B: This is one of the tradeoffs the passage names.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage’s entire caution depends on rejecting this. Historical interest does not erase privacy.
explanations.D: The passage says instructions from the dead are not always decisive.
